Title: Oximes: Unrecognized Chameleons in General and Specialized Plant Metabolism

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides electrons to CYP79A1 and CYP71E1 and supports dhurrin biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. POR supplies the reducing equivalents required for the P450 catalytic cycle in dhurrin biosynthesis. 2. It is part of the dhurrin metabolon model.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Support protein rather than catalyst of product-forming carbon skeleton conversion
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important accessory component for reconstructing P450-rich pathways

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Oxime biosynthesis from amino acids; entry step to cyanogenic glucoside, glucosinolate, auxin and other oxime-derived pathways
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts amino acids into oximes via successive N-hydroxylation, dehydration and decarboxylation chemistry
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Oximes; auxin; cyanogenic glucosides; glucosinolates; camalexin; volatile nitriles
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Growth regulation; defense; pollinator attraction; plant communication; reduced nitrogen storage
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Various tissues including leaves, flowers and defensive/storage tissues depending on product
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Generates amino acid-derived oximes that act as central metabolic bifurcation intermediates toward multiple nutrient- and defense-related products
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP79 enzymes are the conserved direct route for amino acid-derived oxime formation in plants. 2. CYP79 products feed both general metabolism such as auxin formation and specialized metabolism such as cyanogenic glucosides and glucosinolates. 3. The family is widespread in flowering plants and represents the biochemical blueprint for oxime production.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Often induced by herbivory or jasmonic acid for volatile oxime production; otherwise pathway- and tissue-dependent
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: CYP71 family enzymes; CYP736 family enzymes; CYP83 family enzymes; cytochrome P450 oxidoreductase
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Herbivory; jasmonic acid; light/temperature indirectly via downstream pathways
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression; microsomal enzyme assays; isotope/mechanistic studies; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review-level summary across many species; individual CYP79 members differ in substrate specificity and pathway outcome
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May channel flux to defense metabolites at cost of other metabolic pools; free oximes are bioactive and potentially self-toxic
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful entry-point genes for engineering oxime-derived defense compounds, auxin-related flux, and volatile traits

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Initial oxime-forming step of dhurrin pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts L-tyrosine to (E)-p-hydroxyphenylacetaldoxime through two N-hydroxylations followed by dehydration and decarboxylation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense; storage of reduced nitrogen/carbon; possible ROS quenching via turnover products
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables formation of the oxime precursor required for dhurrin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP79A1 was the first characterized CYP79 and establishes the oxime-forming reaction from amino acids in plants. 2. It specifically forms the E-isomer of p-hydroxyphenylacetaldoxime from tyrosine. 3. It functions within a dhurrin biosynthetic metabolon together with CYP71E1, UGT85B1 and POR.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ROS/fungal attack may disrupt downstream metabolon behavior
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Microsomal assays; mutant analysis (tcd1 inactive CYP79A1); mechanistic isotope studies
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: All intermediates are tightly bound in active site, supporting stereospecific E-oxime formation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Cyanogenic defense entails self-toxicity risk if channeling fails
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for engineering dhurrin and related cyanogenic glucoside pathways

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Second P450 step after oxime formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Rearranges E-oxime to Z-oxime, dehydrates to nitrile and hydroxylates to cyanohydrin p-hydroxymandelonitrile
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms immediate cyanohydrin precursor of target product
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Drives conversion of the oxime into cyanohydrin, directing flux to dhurrin rather than other oxime-derived products
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP71E1 catalyzes the unusual conversion of E-oxime through Z-oxime and nitrile intermediates to p-hydroxymandelonitrile. 2. This step distinguishes cyanogenic glucoside biosynthesis from glucosinolate biosynthesis. 3. The enzyme acts within the dhurrin metabolon and is relatively labile under ROS-associated stress.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ROS/fungal attack may denature the enzyme and release oxime intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Microsomal assays; pathway reconstruction; metabolon studies
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Performs multiple chemically unusual reactions not typical for canonical P450s
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Lability may expose toxic intermediates if pathway channeling is disrupted
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important for reconstructing complete cyanogenic glucoside pathways and tuning oxime fate

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Glucosylates the labile cyanohydrin p-hydroxymandelonitrile to form dhurrin
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Stabilizes the labile cyanohydrin as dhurrin by glucosylation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UGT85B1 catalyzes the last step in dhurrin biosynthesis. 2. It converts unstable p-hydroxymandelonitrile to stable dhurrin. 3. It participates in the dhurrin metabolon with CYP79A1, CYP71E1 and POR.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal determinant not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review highlights dhurrin pathway metabolon formation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Required for complete dhurrin pathway engineering

Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Tryptophan N-monooxygenase/oxime-forming cytochromes P450 CYP79B2 and CYP79B3
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Indole-3-acetaldoxime pathway feeding auxin, indole glucosinolate and camalexin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Creates the key metabolic bifurcation intermediate linking general auxin biosynthesis to specialized indole glucosinolate and camalexin formation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP79B2/B3 generate indole-3-acetaldoxime from tryptophan. 2. This oxime is the shared precursor for IAA, indole glucosinolates and camalexin. 3. The step is a prominent example of oximes acting as branchpoints between general and specialized metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Pathogen defense context for camalexin; developmental auxin context
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: IAA can also be produced by routes not involving indole-3-acetaldoxime
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux competition among hormone and defense branches
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Valuable node for tuning growth-defense balance

Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Oxime-metabolizing cytochromes P450 CYP83A1 and CYP83B1
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: E-oximes including indole-3-acetaldoxime and p-hydroxyphenylacetaldoxime-related oximes
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts E-oximes directly into the next glucosinolate pathway intermediates without E-to-Z isomerization
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Commits E-oxime intermediates to glucosinolate biosynthesis and defines divergence from cyanogenic glucoside chemistry
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP83 enzymes metabolize E-oximes in glucosinolate biosynthesis. 2. They do not isomerize E-oximes to Z-oximes. 3. CYP83B1 mutant studies revealed indole-3-acetaldoxime-mediated crosstalk between glucosinolate and phenylpropanoid pathways.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light and temperature influence glucosinolate accumulation at pathway level
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review specifically notes CYP83B1 inability to form Z-oxime, preserving E-oxime structural logic in glucosinolates
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Changes in this step may perturb auxin/indole defense balance
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for redirecting oxime flux into glucosinolate accumulation

Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Chain-elongated methionine N-monooxygenases CYP79F1 and CYP79F2
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Initial oxime-forming step from chain-elongated methionine derivatives
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts chain-elongated methionine derivatives into corresponding aldoximes for aliphatic glucosinolate biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Initiates biosynthesis of methionine-derived aliphatic glucosinolates
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP79F1/F2 catalyze oxime formation from chain-elongated methionine derivatives. 2. Their products feed the aliphatic glucosinolate branch. 3. They exemplify glucosinolate-specialized CYP79 diversification.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Glucosinolate biosynthesis is influenced by light and temperature at system level
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene characterization and pathway assignment cited in review
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Target for engineering aliphatic glucosinolate profiles

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts phenylalanine into phenylacetaldoxime for benzylglucosinolate biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies phenylacetaldoxime precursor for benzylglucosinolate formation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP79A2 is assigned to phenylalanine-derived benzylglucosinolate biosynthesis. 2. It exemplifies aromatic amino acid use by CYP79s in glucosinolate pathways.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional gene assignment summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for altering aromatic glucosinolate content

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts phenylalanine and tryptophan into phenylacetaldoxime and indole-3-acetaldoxime
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Benzyl cyanide; indole-3-acetic acid; volatile oximes
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Herbivore-induced defense and communication; growth regulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Links herbivory-induced oxime accumulation with increased IAA and volatile defense chemistry
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP79A61 produces both phenylacetaldoxime and indole-3-acetaldoxime in maize. 2. Herbivory upregulates CYP79A61 transcript abundance. 3. Increased oxime production is accompanied by increased IAA, illustrating dual general/specialized metabolic function.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Additional CYP71-clan P450s for nitrile formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene expression analysis; metabolite profiling; functional characterization summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Presented in review as example of dual-use CYP79 enzyme across general and specialized metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter auxin levels while enhancing defense volatiles
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising target for engineering inducible defense volatiles with attention to auxin-related pleiotropy

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes conversion in the camalexin branch downstream of indole-3-acetaldoxime
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Channels indole-3-acetaldoxime into the camalexin defense branch
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP71A13 acts downstream of indole-3-acetaldoxime in camalexin biosynthesis. 2. It exemplifies oxime branch diversification toward pathogen-induced defense metabolites.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional pathway studies summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Immediate product not specified in the review text provided
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Competition with auxin and glucosinolate branches
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Could be used to enhance pathogen-inducible camalexin production

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Participates with CYP82C2 in formation of 4-hydroxyindole-3-carbonyl nitrile from the indole-3-acetaldoxime branch
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Strengthens immunity especially to bacterial infections
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes accumulation of an indole-derived immune metabolite from oxime-derived precursors
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP71A12 contributes to synthesis of 4-hydroxyindole-3-carbonyl nitrile. 2. This branch derives from the indole-3-acetaldoxime oxime node. 3. The product supports antibacterial immunity.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional pathway studies summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Product branch noted as oxime-derived defense chemistry beyond camalexin
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to improve disease resistance

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acts with CYP71A12 to form 4-hydroxyindole-3-carbonyl nitrile in oxime-derived immune metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports formation of an oxime-derived antibacterial metabolite
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP82C2 acts with CYP71A12 in the indole-derived defense branch. 2. The resulting metabolite strengthens immunity to bacterial infection.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional pathway studies summarized in review

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts leucine to 3-methylbutyraldoxime in biosynthesis of barley hydroxynitrile glucosides
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Initiates biosynthesis of barley hydroxynitrile glucosides with antifungal activity
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP79A8/A12 produce the leucine-derived oxime precursor in barley hydroxynitrile glucoside biosynthesis. 2. Their pathway products have strong effects against fungal infection.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Products are beta- and gamma-hydroxynitrile glucosides rather than classical cyanogenic glucosides
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to increase antifungal hydroxynitrile glucosides

Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Oxime-metabolizing cytochromes P450 CYP71C113, CYP71L1 and CYP71U5
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyze subsequent dehydration of the oxime to produce the hydroxynitrile in barley hydroxynitrile glucoside biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Convert oxime intermediates further toward bioactive hydroxynitrile glucosides
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP71C113, CYP71L1 and CYP71U5 act after CYP79A8/A12 in barley. 2. They catalyze oxime dehydration steps producing hydroxynitriles. 3. The resulting glucosides contribute to fungal resistance.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Grouped here because the review discusses them together
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful with CYP79A8/A12 for pathway engineering
